title: Prevalence and Correlates of Emotion Dysregulation in Children: Results from the ABCD Study.
journal: Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology
abstract: BACKGROUND: Emotion dysregulation (ED) is the inability to modulate the intensity and quality of emotional responses. It is strongly associated with psychopathology among youth, carries significant morbidity and predicts long-term impairments. Data on ED are limited among nonclinical populations. This study aims to estimate the prevalence of ED and to identify its demographic and clinical correlates in children. METHODS: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, N = 11,878 children (ages 9-10), were analyzed. ED was measured using cutoff scores of â¥180 and <210 on the sum of the Attention, Aggression, and Anxious/Depressed subscales of the Child Behavior Checklist. Demographic and socioeconomic variables, sleep-related variables and physical activity levels, screen time use, and psychopathology were examined as correlates. RESULTS: 9.9% of participants screened positive for ED (moderate 8.4%, severe 1.5%) with a male predominance (60.9% and 64.8% of those who screened positive for moderate and severe ED, respectively). Moderate ED was associated with parental education (adjusted odds ratio [aOR] = 0.716 for graduate university degree vs. bachelor's degree, p = 0.004). Financial hardships such as inability to afford food (aOR = 1.389, p = 0.012) and medical/dental services (aOR = 1.271, p = 0.035) were correlated with moderate ED, while severe ED was associated with lower perceived neighborhood safety (aOR = 1.583, p = 0.024). Psychiatric disorders (current and past), including mood, anxiety, post-traumatic stress, and behavioral disorders, suicidal/homicidal thoughts/behaviors, self-harm, and sleep disturbance, were linked to both moderate and severe ED, while psychosis was linked to severe ED (aOR = 2.873, p < 0.001). CONCLUSION: Psychiatric disorders, socioeconomic factors, and sleep disturbances are important correlates of ED. Findings highlight the importance of early screening for ED and call for the implementation of emotion regulation skills during childhood as a strategic, evidence-based preventive intervention focus.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=11,878 children, ages 9-10 at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen time use' examined as correlate of emotion dysregulation but does not specify whether this is smartphone-specific, social media-specific, or generic screen time composite. Insufficient detail to determine if meets FT-IC2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract does not clearly describe the statistical tests linking screen time (if separately measured) to emotion dysregulation outcomes. Cannot verify inferential test structure.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract focuses on psychiatric/psychosocial correlates with numeric results (aOR, p-values) but no specific numeric results for screen time associations are provided in abstract

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD Study data examining correlates of emotion dysregulation

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology, 2026, DOI 10.1177/10445463251415488

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine; insufficient information in abstract about exposure specification and outcome direction. Recommend PDF review to clarify screen time measurement specificity.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study data, N=11,878 children ages 9-10

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen time use' as examined variable but does not specify smartphone, social media, or any qualifying phone/SM exposure. Generic 'screen time' without device/app clarification fails FT-IC2

FT_IC3.evidence: No phone/SM variable specified; cannot assess association. FT-IC3 depends on FT-IC2 MET

FT_IC4.evidence: No phone/SM numeric coefficient reported; dependent on FT-IC2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional ABCD analysis with reported statistical tests and ORs

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article (Journal of child and adolescent psychopharmacology, 2026); no duplicate evidence

C2.FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply because paper failed FT-IC2 (no smartphone/SM exposure). Paper is excluded at inclusion stage

title: Associations between adverse childhood experiences and early adolescent problematic screen use in the United States.
abstract: BACKGROUND: Problematic screen use, defined as an inability to control use despite private, social, and professional life consequences, is increasingly common among adolescents and can have significant mental and physical health consequences. Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACEs) are important risk factors in the development of addictive behaviors and may play an important role in the development of problematic screen use. METHODS: Prospective data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Baseline and Year 2; 2018-2020; Nâ=â9,673, participants who did not use screens were excluded) were analyzed in 2023. Generalized logistic mixed effects models were used to determine associations with ACEs and the presence of problematic use among adolescents who used screens based on cutoff scores. Secondary analyses used generalized linear mixed effects models to determine associations between ACEs and adolescent-reported problematic use scores of video games (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), social media (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), and mobile phones (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). Analyses were adjusted for potential confounders including age, sex, race/ethnicity, highest parent education, household income, adolescent anxiety, depression, and attention-deficit symptoms, study site, and participants who were twins. RESULTS: The 9,673 screen-using adolescents ages 11-12 years old (mean age 12.0) were racially and ethnically diverse (52.9% White, 17.4% Latino/Hispanic, 19.4% Black, 5.8% Asian, 3.7% Native American, 0.9% Other). Problematic screen use rates among adolescents were identified to be 7.0% (video game), 3.5% (social media), and 21.8% (mobile phone). ACEs were associated with higher problematic video game and mobile phone use in both unadjusted and adjusted models, though problematic social media use was associated with mobile screen use in the unadjusted model only. Adolescents exposed to 4 or more ACEs experienced 3.1 times higher odds of reported problematic video game use and 1.6 times higher odds of problematic mobile phone use compared to peers with no ACEs. CONCLUSIONS: Given the significant associations between adolescent ACE exposure and rates of problematic video and mobile phone screen use among adolescents who use screens, public health programming for trauma-exposed youth should explore video game, social media, and mobile phone use among this population and implement interventions focused on supporting healthy digital habits.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, Baseline and Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9,673 (prospective design)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three specific screen use variables: problematic social media use (SMAQ), problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ), and problematic video game use (VGAQ), all assessed via validated addiction questionnaires

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized logistic mixed effects models for binary outcomes (problematic use yes/no) and generalized linear mixed effects models for continuous problematic use scores; multiple imputation by chained equations for missing data

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports odds ratios with 95% CIs across multiple models; e.g., for problematic video game use with 4+ ACEs: adjusted OR=3.11 (95% CI 2.05-4.73); for mobile phone: OR=1.57 (95% CI 1.22-2.03)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis examining baseline ACE exposure predicting Year 2 problematic screen use outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Public Health, 2023, not a preprint or duplicate

C1.FT_EC7_reason: Although design is prospective (longitudinal) with demographic predictor (ACE), the analysis includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (anxiety, depression, attention-deficit symptoms from CBCL), which are specifically excluded from FT-EC7 applicability per criterion (b)

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data prospectively from Baseline and Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9,673 early adolescents (screen users)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three smartphone/screen modalities: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ). All are specific phone/SM variables assessing problematic use.

FT_IC3.evidence: Generalized logistic mixed effects models and generalized linear mixed effects models testing prospective associations between ACE score (demographic/contextual predictor) and problematic video game use, social media use, and mobile phone use. Multiple tests reported with ORs and continuous scores.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple ORs reported in Table 2: ACEs ≥4 associated with 3.1 times higher odds (2.05-4.73) of problematic video game use and 1.57 times higher odds (1.22-2.03) of problematic mobile phone use

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using prospective design with logistic and linear mixed effects models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in BMC Public Health, 2023, DOI:10.1186/s12889-023-16111-x; not a preprint or duplicate

C2.FT_EC7_reason: All analyses have problematic screen use as the DEPENDENT variable (binary outcomes: problematic video game/SM/phone use). Predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (ACE scores, age, sex, race/ethnicity, income, parent education, mental health covariates for confounding). Design is PROSPECTIVE/LONGITUDINAL (Baseline ACEs predicting Year 2 problematic screen use). All three FT-EC7 conditions are met.

title: Higher recreational screen time and lower step count are associated with higher cardiovascular disease risk in early adolescence
abstract: Adolescence is a critical period for developing behaviors that affect lifelong cardiovascular health. While physical activity improves cardiovascular outcomes, excessive recreational screen time is linked to negative cardiovascular indicators. This study examined associations between screen time and physical activity with cardiovascular risk factors in a large, diverse sample of early adolescents. This study included 4,443 adolescents from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study collected at Year 2 (2018â2020) and Year 4 (2020â2022). Screen time was self-reported and categorized as low (0â4), medium (> 4â8), or high (> 8) hours/day. Physical activity was measured via Fitbit step count over 3 weeks and categorized as low (1,000â6,000), medium (> 6,000â12,000), and high (> 12,000) steps/day. Outcomes included blood pressure percentile and hypertensive-range blood pressure, which were available for the full sample, while total cholesterol, high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, non-HDL cholesterol, hemoglobin A1c, and testing consistent with diabetes were available in a subset (one-third) at Year 4. Adjusted linear and Poisson regression models examined joint associations of screen time and step count with cardiovascular health outcomes. Adolescents averaged 6.1 (Â± 5.1) hours/day of screen use and 9,280 (Â± 3,279) steps/day. Higher screen time and lower step count showed dose-response associations with higher diastolic blood pressure percentile (all p < 0.01). Compared to reference categories, high screen time (> 8 h/day) was associated with a 4.25-point increase in diastolic blood pressure percentile (95% confidence interval [CI] 1.85 to 6.65) and low step count (1,000â6,000 steps/day) was associated with a 7.15-point increase (95% CI 4.82 to 9.48). Both high screen time (adjusted risk ratio [ARR] 1.88, 95% CI 1.42 to 2.56) and low step count (ARR 2.35, 95% CI 1.88 to 2.94) were associated with increased risk of hypertensive-range blood pressure. Low step count was associated with -3.72 mg/dL lower HDL cholesterol (95% CI -6.52 to -2.92) and increased risk of low HDL cholesterol (ARR 2.05, 95% CI 1.79 to 2.36). Higher screen time and lower physical activity were associated with poorer cardiovascular health, supporting guidelines and early interventions promoting increased physical activity and reduced screen time.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data; 4,443 adolescents from ABCD Study Year 2 (2018–2020) and Year 4 (2020–2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure measured as 'recreational screen time' categorized as low (0–4), medium (>4–8), or high (>8) hours/day. This is generic total screen time composite with no smartphone-specific or social-media-specific breakdown. Criteria state: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown' = NOT_MET (FT-IC2 exclusion). Per E17/E18 rules, unspecified screen-time variable → NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC4.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC5.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

FT_IC6.evidence: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; subsequent criteria not assessed due to exposure failure

C1.FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET; paper excluded on exposure criterion. FT-EC7 not evaluated.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (Year 2 [2018-2020] and Year 4 [2020-2022] assessments; N=4,443 adolescents)

FT_IC2.evidence: Recreational screen time measured via self-report and categorized as low (0-4 hours/day), medium (>4-8 hours/day), or high (>8 hours/day)

FT_IC3.evidence: Adjusted linear and Poisson regression models examining associations between screen time categories and cardiovascular disease risk outcomes (blood pressure percentile, hypertensive-range BP, cholesterol levels, hemoglobin A1c)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple coefficients and risk ratios reported: high screen time associated with 4.25-point increase in diastolic BP percentile (95% CI 1.85-6.65), adjusted risk ratio 1.88 (95% CI 1.42-2.56) for hypertensive-range BP, non-HDL cholesterol increase (95% CI 1.79-2.36)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD longitudinal analysis; examined associations of Year 2 screen time and step count with Year 4 cardiovascular outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in BMC Public Health (2026), not a preprint or duplicate

C2.FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable predicting cardiovascular health outcomes (blood pressure, cholesterol, hemoglobin A1c) as dependent variables. This is a screen time→non-SM outcome association, so FT-EC7 exclusion applies.
